name: Hide of the Nemean Lion
description: The Hide of the Nemean Lion is a legendary pelt from the fearsome lion that terrorized the region of Nemea. This lion, a monstrous offspring of Typhon and Echidna (though accounts vary), possessed impenetrable golden fur, making it invulnerable to conventional weapons. Heracles, as his first of twelve labors assigned by King Eurystheus, was tasked with slaying the beast and bringing back its hide. After failing to pierce the lion's skin with arrows and his sword, Heracles resorted to wrestling the lion, eventually strangling it to death. He then attempted to skin the lion but found its claws were the only things sharp enough to cut through its hide. Some versions say Athena advised him to use the lion's own claws to skin it. Once skinned, the hide became an impenetrable armor for Heracles, offering complete protection from weapons and blows. He wore it for many years, often with the lion's head serving as a helmet, a symbol of his unparalleled strength and divine favor. The hide represented not only invulnerability but also the triumph of human strength and ingenuity over seemingly insurmountable odds. It served as a constant reminder of Heracles's heroic deeds and his enduring legacy, becoming one of his most iconic attributes. It is a symbol of raw power and resilience, a testament to the capabilities of a hero blessed by the gods and tested by fate. The hide also instilled fear in his enemies, for they knew they could not harm him while he wore it.
subtitle: Impenetrable Armor of the Hero
origin: Acquired by Heracles after slaying the Nemean Lion as his first labor.
currentLocation: Lost to time; symbolic representation in art and literature

keyMyths.title: The First Labor
keyMyths.description: Heracles is tasked with slaying the Nemean Lion as his first labor. He eventually strangles the lion and skins it using its own claws.

keyMyths.title: Heracles' Armor
keyMyths.description: The hide becomes Heracles's primary armor, offering complete protection from weapons. He wears it throughout his subsequent labors and adventures.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive account of Greek myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive account of Greek myths and legends.

sources.author: Diodorus Siculus
sources.description: A universal history covering various cultures, including Greek mythology.
